She wrote back accepting the terms, choosing her words with the same care she gave to phrases she was paid nothing for.

Mrs. Eddleston said he would do very well. The girls descended. Outside, the evening air smelled of cut grass and something further off, rain perhaps. The coachman did not look at them. They were going to the Pearsons' at last.

I'm very fond of Molly Gibson, very, for her own sake and for her mother's too; I'm not sure if I don't think she's worth half-a-dozen Cynthias, but for all that she shouldn't break my best china teacup, and then sit doing nothing for her livelihood all the rest of the evening."

The girl might wrap herself in a shawl. There were shawls enough in the cedar chest, if she would only take the trouble to look, instead of sitting with that pinched, reproachful face and saying nothing. Silence of that particular kind was a form of accusation, and Dorothea recognized it perfectly, having employed it herself against her own aunt before she was old enough to know better.

His wife kept a lodger, a typewriter girl from the Emporium, and sometimes in the evenings the three of them sat at the kitchen table and talked about wages, about the eight-hour movement, about whether a man's labor belonged to himself or to the company that purchased it by the week.

humanText: A week passed, then a fortnight, then a month. It was a month of the greatest anxiety and unquietude for Trina. McTeague was out of a job, could find nothing to do; and Trina, who saw the impossibility of saving as much money as usual out of her earnings under the present conditions, was on the lookout for cheaper quarters. In spite of his outcries and sulky resistance Trina had induced her husband to consent to such a move, bewildering him with a torrent of phrases and marvellous columns of figures by which she proved conclusively that they were in a condition but one remove from downright destitution.
aiText: Three years on the Barbary Coast had taken something from Connelly that no amount of steady wages could restore. He worked the fish stalls at the Ferry Building now, hauling crates before light, his hands gone raw and salt-white by midmorning. The foreman, a heavyset Irishman named Brauer, docked his pay twice in November for lateness, and Connelly said nothing, only nodded and turned back to the ice. His wife Nora kept the figures in a ledger she hid beneath the floorboards, knowing already what the numbers meant, knowing already what Connelly would do when the cold came properly and the boats stopped running.

Whether he had wept on the morning he left for Manchester she could not now say. She had cried herself, standing at the kitchen door while the cab waited. The neighbours had seen her. That, too, was certain. The rest of it—the farewell, the brave face, the words exchanged on the step—had grown smooth with handling, like a coin worn past reading.

Now one clear idea came into her head. He was at fault. There was no doubt about it. Why did he bring Hurstwood out—Hurstwood, a married man, and never say a word to her? Never mind now about Hurstwood’s perfidy—why had he done this? Why hadn’t he warned her? There he stood now, guilty of this miserable breach of confidence and talking about what he had done for her!
aiText: The city took no notice of him. That was the first thing Selden understood, stepping off the car into the noise of Canal Street. A man could starve here and the buildings would not shift. He had forty cents. He had a collar that was clean on one side. The employment office smelled of wet wool and other men's failure, and the clerk looked at him the way clerks looked at everyone—without seeing anything worth the trouble. What had he expected? He was not a man given to precise self-examination; he had views, naturally, on most subjects—the land question, the education of women, the duties of property—but views held as one holds an umbrella, useful in inclement weather and otherwise forgotten. What troubled him was simpler than principle: she had looked at him across the table with those calm eyes and he had felt, obscurely, that she already knew the answer he was still searching for, and had decided it was not worth the trouble of supplying to him.

humanText: After all, however, there was a dull space of time which needed relieving with bread and cheese and ale, and when he was seated alone with these resources in the wainscoted parlor, he suddenly slapped his knee, and exclaimed, “Ladislaw!” That action of memory which he had tried to set going, and had abandoned in despair, had suddenly completed itself without conscious effort—a common experience, agreeable as a completed sneeze, even if the name remembered is of no value. Her husband would be home at six. She set the kettle on and did not move from the window for a long time, watching the sheets, feeling something in her chest that she could not yet name and was not certain she wished to.

He leaned upon the lounge with an arm extended across her, while the other hand still rested upon her hair. They continued silently to look into each other’s eyes. When he leaned forward and kissed her, she clasped his head, holding his lips to hers.
aiText: She had not spoken her husband's name aloud in three days. This seemed to her a fact worth examining, the way one turns a stone to see what lies beneath. She could not have said when it began. She only knew that the woman she had been last spring felt remote now, like a correspondent who had stopped writing.

Her reflection hovered in the glass, faint and uncommitted. She pressed her palm flat against the cold pane and held it there until she understood that she was waiting for something she could not name.

Martin, coming into her kitchen to fetch water, was invited to sit down and drink. He drank her very-good health, and in return she drank his. Then she drank to prosperity in his undertakings, and he drank to the hope that James Grant would show up and pay her for his washing. James Grant was a journeymen carpenter who did not always pay his bills and who owed Maria three dollars.

humanText: “It is not in what you succeed in doing that you get your joy, but in the doing of it. You can’t tell me. I know it. You know it. Beauty hurts you. It is an everlasting pain in you, a wound that does not heal, a knife of flame. Why should you palter with magazines? Let beauty be your end. Why should you mint beauty into gold? Anyway, you can’t; so there’s no use in my getting excited over it. You can read the magazines for a thousand years and you won’t find the value of one line of Keats. Leave fame and coin alone, sign away on a ship to-morrow, and go back to your sea.”

What he understood, he understood completely. What he didn't, he left alone and circled back. His mother wanted him home. There was work. There was always work. But he had read Emerson by then and something had cracked open that would not close again. He was not interested in being reasonable. He was interested in being educated, which is a different hunger, and far less polite.

humanText: “Men!” I began to answer, rather hotly, and then saw the gulf before me. None of us wanted these women to think that our women, of whom we boasted so proudly, were in any way inferior to them. I am ashamed to say that I equivocated. I told her of certain criminal types of women--perverts, or crazy, who had been known to commit infanticide. I told her, truly enough, that there was much in our land which was open to criticism, but that I hated to dwell on our defects until they understood us and our conditions better.

She looked at him with genuine puzzlement, the way one looks at a man who has asked why water runs downhill. He wrote nothing for several minutes. There was a shame in the question itself that he had only just begun to feel.

They had long since separated the idea of labor from the idea of personal gain, and what remained, once that ancient confusion was cleared away, was something closer to appetite — the same unremarkable drive that causes a person to reach for food when hungry, to sleep when tired, to speak when there is something worth saying.

humanText: “Yeh know Tom Jamison, he lives next door t’ me up home. He’s a nice feller, he is, an’ we was allus good friends. Smart, too. Smart as a steel trap. Well, when we was a-fightin’ this atternoon, all-of-a-sudden he begin t’ rip up an’ cuss an’ beller at me. ‘Yer shot, yeh blamed infernal!’—he swear horrible—he ses t’ me. I put up m’ hand t’ m’ head an’ when I looked at m’ fingers, I seen, sure ’nough, I was shot. I give a holler an’ begin t’ run, but b’fore I could git away another one hit me in th’ arm an’ whirl’ me clean ’round. I got skeared when they was all a-shootin’ b’hind me an’ I run t’ beat all, but I cotch it pretty bad. I’ve an idee I’d a’ been fightin’ yit, if t’wasn’t fer Tom Jamison.”
aiText: The road was mud. It had been mud for three days. Caleb walked it without thinking, the way a man walks when thinking has stopped being useful. Somewhere behind him the guns had gone quiet, which was worse than noise. Holt was dead. He had seen it happen in a way he could not describe and would not try. The boy's coat was still folded under Caleb's arm, wool gone stiff with cold. A woman stood in a farmyard watching them pass. She did not wave. The men did not look at her. There was a smell of something burning that was not wood. Caleb's feet had stopped hurting two miles back, which Sergeant Prewitt said was a bad sign, and Prewitt knew things. The sky was the color of an old wound. Nobody talked. The road went on.

aiText: The gun went off before Pruett knew he had fired it. Smoke. The smell of it, thick as wool against his face. Somebody fell. He could not say if it was his doing or another man's. The line moved forward and he moved with it, not thinking. His feet found the ground and lost it. Elias was shouting something from the left flank but the words came apart in the noise. Then the noise stopped, all at once, the way a door shuts. Pruett stood in a field of broken corn and looked at his hands. They were shaking hard. He had not eaten since morning and his stomach understood this now, suddenly, with great urgency. Around him men sat or lay or walked in slow circles. Nobody spoke much. The October light came flat across the stubble. A horse screamed somewhere past the tree line, then did not scream again.

humanText: “Yeh know Tom Jamison, he lives next door t’ me up home. He’s a nice feller, he is, an’ we was allus good friends. Smart, too. Smart as a steel trap. Well, when we was a-fightin’ this atternoon, all-of-a-sudden he begin t’ rip up an’ cuss an’ beller at me. ‘Yer shot, yeh blamed infernal!’—he swear horrible—he ses t’ me. I put up m’ hand t’ m’ head an’ when I looked at m’ fingers, I seen, sure ’nough, I was shot. I give a holler an’ begin t’ run, but b’fore I could git away another one hit me in th’ arm an’ whirl’ me clean ’round. I got skeared when they was all a-shootin’ b’hind me an’ I run t’ beat all, but I cotch it pretty bad. I’ve an idee I’d a’ been fightin’ yit, if t’wasn’t fer Tom Jamison.”
aiText: Holloway lay in the ditch and watched the sky. It was a pale sky, wrong somehow, like milk left out. The shelling had stopped an hour back or maybe two. Time did queer things out here. Beside him Sergeant Foss breathed in a particular way that Holloway did not like. Wet, it was. Labored. The cornfield ahead showed nothing moving, but that meant little enough. Holloway's hands were cold despite the heat. He thought of his mother's kitchen, the iron stove, the smell of cornbread. Then a man screamed somewhere in the field and the thought broke apart. Foss said something that Holloway couldn't make out. He leaned closer. Foss said it again. It was a woman's name, probably his wife's. The pale sky sat overhead, indifferent. Holloway checked his rifle. He would have to stand up soon.

humanText: The youth wished to launch a joke—a quotation from newspapers. He desired to say, “All quiet on the Rappahannock,” but the guns refused to permit even a comment upon their uproar. He never successfully concluded the sentence. But at last the guns stopped, and among the men in the rifle pits rumors again flew, like birds, but they were now for the most part black creatures who flapped their wings drearily near to the ground and refused to rise on any wings of hope. The men’s faces grew doleful from the interpreting of omens. Tales of hesitation and uncertainty on the part of those high in place and responsibility came to their ears. Stories of disaster were borne into their minds with many proofs. This din of musketry on the right, growing like a released genie of sound, expressed and emphasized the army’s plight.
aiText: The road had gone to mud. Calhoun watched the column ahead slow, compress, stop—men settling into themselves like wet sacks. Word came back that the bridge was burned, the ford unpassable. Nobody argued. A horse screamed somewhere in the gray distance and then did not scream again. The men along the verge spoke little, their mouths closed against the cold. Calhoun had marched four days believing in something he could no longer name. Now he believed only in the mud, the stopped column, the horse that had been silenced. Rain began again, without drama. A lieutenant he did not know moved past and the lieutenant's face held no message except fatigue. Around him the army breathed and shifted and did not advance. The field to the east was smoke, or fog—in that country, by that season, a man could rarely say which.

aiText: Strother had not eaten since the day before. He knew this the way a man knows weather, not by thought but by the body's own gray arithmetic. The regiment moved at a crouch through the corn stubble, and somewhere ahead a skirmisher's rifle spoke once and fell silent. Nobody answered it. The officers had their swords out, which meant nothing good. Around Strother the men breathed through their mouths and watched the tree line as though it owed them something. A horse screamed in the distance and was quiet. The field smelled of wet ash and the particular sweetness that Strother had learned not to name. Sergeant Dodd passed him going the other direction, face emptied of whatever Dodd's face used to hold. The corn stalks stood broken and pale. The order to advance had been given twice now and still the line did not move.

aiText: The cannon had been quiet for an hour and the quiet was worse. Breckinridge lay in the shallow trench and listened to it. Around him men shifted, picked at their equipment, said nothing of consequence. A corporal began to count something on his fingers and then stopped. The smoke still hung in the treeline across the field, unmoving, as though it had decided to stay. Word came back that Holt's brigade had broken on the left. Nobody disputed this. The man beside Breckinridge turned the information over in his mind visibly, his jaw working at nothing. Orders would come or they would not come. The field itself seemed to be waiting for something it did not expect to receive. Breckinridge pressed his back against the clay wall and felt its cold and thought about the particular silence that follows a large decision.

humanText: So, for a time, if such a passion come to fruition, the man will get what he wants. He will get the moral support, the encouragement, the relief from the sense of loneliness, the assurance of his own worth. But these things pass away; inevitably they pass away as the shadows pass across sundials. It is sad, but it is so. The pages of the book will become familiar; the beautiful corner of the road will have been turned too many times. Well, this is the saddest story.
aiText: She had been to the house, she was almost certain of that. The smell of lavender on the stairs — or was it camphor? Something sharp and domestic. Her cousin stood in the doorway, or perhaps her cousin had not come at all and she was confusing one afternoon with another. This was the trouble with remembering. The years did not arrange themselves in order but pooled together, indistinct. She could not say with confidence what she had felt, only that she had felt something.

aiText: He remembers the letter, or believes he does. The handwriting slanted left, which struck him as unusual, though he cannot say why. Perhaps there was no letter. Perhaps it was a telegram, or a conversation on the stairs that he has since arranged into sentences. Memory does its work quietly, like a forger. What remains is the feeling of having known, and then the long, peculiar shame of having forgotten what it was he knew.

aiText: There was an afternoon in September, she thinks, though it may have been October. The light had a particular quality. She had said something to him by the window, something that mattered, and he had turned away. What she said is gone now; only the turning remains. This is how it goes with the past. It keeps the gesture and loses the word, keeps the wound and loses the cause of it.

humanText: I do not know much about English legal procedure—I cannot, I mean, give technical details of how they tied him up. But I know that, two days later, without her having said more than I have reported to you, Leonora and her attorney had become the trustees, as I believe it is called, of all Edward's property, and there was an end of Edward as the good landlord and father of his people. He went out.
aiText: I cannot say with certainty when Florence first mentioned the house, or whether it was Florence at all. Memory arranges these things badly. What I do recall—and here I may be trusted, or nearly trusted—is that by the following Thursday the lease had been signed by someone, and we were standing in those dim rooms with their smell of old paper, already pretending it had been our intention from the beginning.

aiText: The letter, I believe, arrived on a Thursday. Or it may have been the Friday — Maud would correct me here, but Maud is gone now and cannot. What I know is that Pemberton read it twice, folded it into his breast pocket, and said nothing to anyone for three days. Whether he was frightened or simply calculating, I have never been able to determine, and I stopped trying some years ago.

humanText: And, when they were in the street, he chatted as naturally as usual. Unquestionably he was not a man of jealous disposition, or else he was too good-natured to get angry. Besides, his time was devoted to serving his country. He never left off his uniform now. On the twenty-ninth of March he had defended the offices of the Presse. When the Chamber was invaded, he distinguished himself by his courage, and he was at the banquet given to the National Guard at Amiens.
aiText: Forestier received the news without visible agitation. He was a man who had long since made peace with disappointment, or perhaps had simply ceased to notice it. He folded the letter and placed it beneath his inkwell. That evening he dined well, spoke of the railway question, and laughed twice at something Norbert said. His wife watched him from across the table. She could not decide whether he was admirable or merely hollow.

aiText: Madame Vernet did not weep at the funeral. Her sister found this scandalous and said so plainly. But grief had its own arrangements, and she had made hers privately, in the blue hour before the servants rose. The house in the rue Saint-Honoré was sold by October. She took a small apartment near the Luxembourg and began receiving on Thursdays, which everyone agreed was too soon.

aiText: Rougon had not visited the apartment since February. The concierge told him Madame Laure had gone to her sister's in Rouen, but this was a lie she had rehearsed. He climbed to the third floor regardless. The door was locked. He stood there a full minute, hat in hand, listening to someone move about inside. Then he descended, crossed the courtyard, and took the omnibus back toward the Palais-Royal, where he had a dinner engagement he had entirely forgotten.

"He is not likely to die;" and at dessert he had a sort of fainting fit. A doctor was at once sent for, and he prescribed a potion. Then, when M. Roque was in bed, he asked to be as well wrapped up as possible in order to bring on perspiration. He gasped; he moaned.
aiText: Madame Verlet had not slept. The candle on her nightstand had burned to a disk of wax, and the letter from her cousin remained folded beside it. She dressed without calling the maid. Downstairs, the concierge was already awake, sweeping the courtyard with long indifferent strokes. She passed him without speaking. The street smelled of rain and horses. She had decided nothing, and yet her feet carried her steadily toward the rue Saint-Jacques, as though her body had resolved what her mind refused.

aiText: The notary arrived at half past ten and found the shutters still closed. Beaumont let him in without a word. On the table lay three letters, none of them opened. The elder Dufresne had died on a Tuesday, which struck everyone as irregular somehow. His daughter wept in the garden while her husband spoke of the harvest. A nephew from Rouen had already inquired about the clock. The notary set down his case, accepted a glass of water, and began to read.

aiText: Édouard had been walking since morning. He stopped at a café near the prefecture and ordered coffee, which he did not drink. The proprietor watched him from behind the counter. There was a letter in his coat pocket, and he was aware of it the way a man is aware of a wound. His sister had written that the house was sold. He paid and went out into the street. The rain had begun again.
